summary: Jeff Atwood discusses his discovery of better visual diff tools while building a .NET library for creating MHT (single-file web archive) files. He needed to compare his library's output against IE6's native MHT generation and found Visual SourceSafe's built-in comparison tool inadequate. After researching alternatives, he settled on Araxis Merge as his preferred visual comparison tool, despite its high price. He laments that VSS doesn't support external diff tools for Visual Studio integration.
key_points: MHT files follow the standard Multipart MIME Message RFC rather than a proprietary Microsoft format
Visual SourceSafe's built-in diff tool is inadequate for serious comparison work
Every developer should have a quality diff tool in their toolkit
Araxis Merge is recommended as a professional visual comparison tool despite its cost
Tool limitations in VSS prevent integration with better external comparison tools
money_quotes: Every developer should have a good diff tool in their toolkit.
Surprisingly, instead of some crazy proprietary Microsoft format like you'd expect, the file follows the simple Multipart MIME Message RFC standard.
Building an .MHT file is sort of like sending an email to yourself – go figure.
It's workable, but it's kind of... ghetto.
If like me, the only diff tool you ever used was the one in VSS – you may not know how much you're missing.
key_insight: Developers often settle for inadequate default tools without realizing how much better alternatives exist, and investing in quality developer tools pays dividends in productivity.
